content.text: A tool registry is a runtime store that maps tool names to their definitions. Instead of hard-coding a fixed list of tools at agent startup, the registry lets you add, remove, and query tools dynamically — so the set of tools passed to the model on each API call can vary by conversation context, user role, or plugin configuration without redeploying the agent.

attrs.alt: Tool registry lifecycle: agent lookups, static and dynamic tool sources
attrs.title: Tool Registry Dynamic Lifecycle

content.text: A registry is not mandatory for small agents with five tools. It becomes valuable when the tool set grows past what any one conversation should see, when tools are loaded from plugins, or when different user roles need different capabilities.

A registry stores tool definitions keyed by name and exposes a small query interface. The tool definition is a dataclass that bundles the schema (what the model sees) with the handler (what gets called).
method separates the model-facing contract from the internal handler. The registry holds both; the API call receives only the schema.
Tools are registered once at startup. A decorator makes registration ergonomic and keeps the schema close to the handler.

A support agent should not expose billing mutation tools to a read-only session, and an admin agent should not hand its full tool set to a lower-privilege user. Build the per-turn tool list from the registry using tags, role, or conversation context.
list changes per call based on role — the same registry serves every role, but the model only sees the subset it is allowed to call. The dispatcher uses
to look up the handler; if the model somehow names a tool outside its allowed set, the registry returns
and the loop returns an error.

A plugin system lets tools be defined in separate files or packages and loaded into the registry at startup (or on demand) without changing the agent's core code. The simplest form uses Python's entry points or a directory scan.

import importlib
import importlib.util
import sys
from pathlib import Path

def load_plugin_directory(plugin_dir: str | Path, registry: ToolRegistry) -> int:
    """
    Import every .py file in plugin_dir. Each file is expected to call
    registry.register() for its tools. Returns the number of files loaded.
    """
    plugin_dir = Path(plugin_dir)
    count = 0
    for path in sorted(plugin_dir.glob("*.py")):
        if path.name.startswith("_"):
            continue
        module_name = f"_plugin_{path.stem}"
        spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(module_name, path)
        if spec is None or spec.loader is None:
            continue
        module = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
        sys.modules[module_name] = module
        # Pass the shared registry so the plugin can register into it
        module.registry = registry
        spec.loader.exec_module(module)   # type: ignore[union-attr]
        count += 1
    return count

# Usage at startup:
# loaded = load_plugin_directory("plugins/", registry)
# print(f"{loaded} plugin files loaded, {len(registry.list_all())} tools registered")
Each plugin file receives the shared registry object and calls
for its tools. A plugin for the billing system, for example, registers
; the role-based filter in the agent loop then controls who sees them.
A more advanced pattern fetches tool definitions from a remote catalog service rather than from local files. This lets multiple agents share a single source of truth for tool schemas.

import requests as req
import importlib

CATALOG_URL = "https://catalog.internal.corp/tools"

def load_from_catalog(
    registry: ToolRegistry,
    tags: list[str] | None = None,
    handler_module: str = "mytools.handlers",
) -> int:
    """
    Pull tool definitions from the catalog API and register them.
    The catalog returns schemas; handlers are resolved from handler_module.
    """
    params = {"tags": ",".join(tags)} if tags else {}
    resp = req.get(CATALOG_URL, params=params, timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    entries = resp.json()   # [{name, description, input_schema, tags}]

    handlers_mod = importlib.import_module(handler_module)
    loaded = 0
    for entry in entries:
        handler = getattr(handlers_mod, entry["name"], None)
        if handler is None:
            continue   # schema in catalog but no local handler — skip
        registry.register(ToolDef(
            name=entry["name"],
            description=entry["description"],
            input_schema=entry["input_schema"],
            handler=handler,
            tags=entry.get("tags", []),
        ))
        loaded += 1
    return loaded

The catalog owns schemas; the local module owns handlers. The split means a schema change (description update, new parameter) can be deployed in the catalog without touching the agent code — the agent picks it up on next restart.
Not extensible; model sees all tools always
Tool sets that vary by deployment
Plugin files must be on the same host

import pytest
from mytools.registry import ToolRegistry, ToolDef

@pytest.fixture
def reg():
    r = ToolRegistry()
    r.register(ToolDef(
        name="ping",
        description="Return pong.",
        input_schema={"type":"object","properties":{}},
        handler=lambda: {"result": "pong"},
        tags=["read", "test"],
    ))
    r.register(ToolDef(
        name="write_log",
        description="Write a log entry.",
        input_schema={"type":"object","properties":{"msg":{"type":"string"}}},
        handler=lambda msg: {"written": True},
        tags=["write", "test"],
    ))
    return r

def test_get_existing(reg):
    assert reg.get("ping") is not None

def test_get_missing_returns_none(reg):
    assert reg.get("nonexistent") is None

def test_filter_by_tag_read(reg):
    read_tools = reg.filter_by_tags("read")
    assert all("read" in t.tags for t in read_tools)
    assert len(read_tools) == 1

def test_search(reg):
    results = reg.search("log")
    assert any(t.name == "write_log" for t in results)

def test_duplicate_registration_raises(reg):
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="already registered"):
        reg.register(ToolDef(
            name="ping",
            description="duplicate",
            input_schema={},
            handler=lambda: {},
        ))

def test_unregister(reg):
    reg.unregister("ping")
    assert reg.get("ping") is None

def test_to_api_schema_excludes_handler(reg):
    schema = reg.get("ping").to_api_schema()
    assert "handler" not in schema
    assert schema["name"] == "ping"
A tool registry decouples tool definition from tool usage — the agent loop asks the registry for tools at runtime rather than maintaining a hard-coded list.
Tag-based filtering lets the same registry serve multiple roles: a read-only session gets
Plugin directories allow tools to be added without modifying the agent's core code — each plugin file registers itself into the shared registry at load time.
A remote catalog separates schema ownership (catalog service) from handler ownership (local code); schema updates deploy without a code change.
Always verify that the model can only call tools in its allowed subset — if
, return an error rather than raising an exception.
